student_name: Eliane Portela Nogueira Lima de Brito
hub_subtitle: Business English for a doctor rebuilding her professional voice

lesson.menu_title: Reading the Market — Company Profiles and the Numbers Behind Them
lesson.menu_desc: Relative clauses (defining and non-defining) for describing companies, people and markets, built on a real market-profile reading -- 31 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 10 -- Reading the Market
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Eliane Portela | Aula 10 -- Reading the Market: Company Profiles

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: a
the established leader that already dominates a market
b
the percentage of a market that one company controls
c
a small, specialized part of a market with specific needs
d
the profit left after costs are subtracted
e
the ongoing running costs of a business, such as rent and staff
f
the advantage that makes a company hard to beat
g
a distinct source of regular income for a business
h
to use something you have to get the most value from it
i
a standard used to compare and measure performance
j
the full range of products or services a company offers
k
the careful research done before a deal or investment
l
the estimated financial worth of a company

paras: Every founder who launches a clinic eventually has to read the market, not just the medicine. Eliane, whose clinic will open next year, has started to study the companies that already compete in fertility care. The largest of them, which analysts call the incumbent, holds the biggest market share; but its high overhead and thin margins make it slow to change. Around it sits a ring of smaller players, each of which has found a narrow niche that the big group ignores.

The most interesting profile is a start-up whose whole strategy rests on one competitive edge: technology that lowers the cost of each cycle. Its founders, who raised seed funding two years ago, now report a valuation that surprised even their investors. Analysts who follow the sector point to a new revenue stream -- data and software -- that the incumbent has been slow to build. In a report that circulated last month, one investor wrote that any group which cannot leverage technology will lose ground within five years.

For Eliane, the lesson is clear. Before she signs with any partner, she will do the due diligence that every serious founder does: she will read each company's numbers, compare them against an industry benchmark, and study the portfolio of services that made the leaders grow. The clinic that she is building does not need to be the biggest. It needs a niche it can defend, a margin it can sustain, and a competitive edge that patients can feel.
reading.source: Adapted from a market-analysis case study, The Founder's Read

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Eliane has decided to buy the incumbent and become the largest company in the fertility-care market.

To succeed, Eliane is learning to read her market -- studying competitors' profiles and numbers -- and concludes her clinic needs a defensible niche, a sustainable margin, and a real competitive edge, not size.
The text argues that technology does not matter in fertility care and that the incumbent will always win.

items: The incumbent holds the biggest market share but is slow to change.
The text says the incumbent holds the biggest market share, but its high overhead and thin margins make it slow to change.
The start-up's competitive edge is technology that lowers the cost of each cycle.
The text says the start-up's strategy rests on one competitive edge: technology that lowers the cost of each cycle.
The text says Eliane wants her clinic to become the biggest company in the market.
The text says the clinic does not need to be the biggest -- it needs a niche, a margin, and a competitive edge.
Analysts say the incumbent has already built a strong new revenue stream in data and software.
The text says the incumbent has been slow to build that revenue stream; the start-up leads it.

gapfill.text: Notice how the words work together: you do your due diligence, compare against a benchmark, measure a competitor's market share and margin, and then position your own niche, portfolio and competitive edge. This is the language a founder uses to read a market.

bank.label: Your market-reading toolkit
items: The clinic that I am building has a niche it can defend.
The investor who funded us now sits on the board.
Our supplier, whose terms are fair, delivers on time.
The market, which is growing fast, rewards focus.
A company that cannot leverage technology will fall behind.
The strategy which made them grow is simple: one competitive edge.
Analysts who follow the sector expect the margins to widen.
The report, which circulated last month, questioned the valuation.

lf.title: Defining or non-defining? Read the relative clause
items: raised the funding is my mentor.
terms are fair, delivers on time.
follows the sector, wrote the report.

lf.text: A and C and D are defining clauses -- no commas, and the clause is essential (who/that/which). B and E are non-defining -- commas around extra information (who/which/whose, but never that). Rule of thumb: if you can drop the clause and still know exactly who or what you mean, it is non-defining, so use commas.

items.situation: Describe the market leader in your field in one sentence. Use a relative clause and the word 'incumbent' or 'market share'.
tips: The incumbent, which holds the biggest market share, is slow to change.
The company that leads our market has high overhead and thin margins.

items.situation: Explain what makes a start-up different. Use 'competitive edge' and a relative clause.
tips: Their competitive edge is a technology that lowers the cost of each cycle.
The start-up, whose strategy is focused, found a niche the big group ignored.

items.situation: Say what you check before signing with a partner. Use 'due diligence' and 'benchmark'.
tips: I do the due diligence that every serious founder does.
I compare their numbers against a benchmark that the industry trusts.

items.situation: Describe your own clinic's strategy. Use 'niche', 'margin' and a relative clause.
tips: The clinic that I am building has a niche it can defend.
We want a margin which is sustainable and a portfolio patients value.

items: Describe the incumbent and one smaller competitor in your field. Use market share, overhead, and at least two defining relative clauses (who / which / that).
Profile a start-up that is changing your market. Explain its competitive edge and its new revenue stream, using non-defining clauses ("..., which..., ..., whose...").
Walk through the checks you would run before signing with a partner: the benchmark you compare against, the portfolio you study, the valuation you question.
In one minute, describe your clinic's strategy: the niche it defends, the margin it sustains, and the competitive edge patients feel. Use as many relative clauses as you can.

answerkey.title: Reveal the model language
list: Map the market: 'The incumbent, which holds the biggest market share, has high overhead. Around it are smaller clinics, each of which has found a niche.'
The disruptor: 'The start-up, whose strategy is focused, has one competitive edge: technology that lowers costs. Its new revenue stream, which is data and software, is growing fast.'
Due diligence: 'I do the due diligence that every founder does -- I compare their numbers against an industry benchmark, and I study the portfolio of services that made the leaders grow.'
Your strategy: 'The clinic that I am building has a niche it can defend, a margin it can sustain, and a competitive edge that patients can feel.'
answerkey.note: Notice how every strategic point rides on a relative clause -- it is the structure that lets you add detail without starting a new sentence.

listenings.text: Hi Eliane, it is Paul. I have looked at the market profile you sent, and here is my quick read. The incumbent, which still holds the biggest market share, is not your real problem. Its overhead is high and its margins are thin, so it moves slowly. The company that should interest you is the start-up whose valuation jumped last year. Their competitive edge is a technology that lowers the cost of each cycle, and they have opened a revenue stream, data and software, that no one else offers. My advice is simple: do not try to beat the incumbent on size. Find the niche that you can defend, and do the due diligence that protects your margin. The clinic that wins is the one that knows exactly where it competes.

Hi team, it is Eliane. I want to recap what I learned reading our market this week. First, the leader, the incumbent that everyone watches, has the biggest market share, but its high overhead makes it slow. Second, the start-up whose funding we discussed has one clear competitive edge: technology that cuts the cost per cycle. Analysts who follow the sector say its new revenue stream is the future. Here is our takeaway: we will not compete on size. We will do the due diligence that serious founders do, compare ourselves against a real benchmark, and build a portfolio of services that gives us a margin we can sustain and a niche we can defend. That is the strategy which will carry us.
